plain: English is the most common language spoken world-wide and it is one of the easiest languages to learn and speak. This book is all about developing the very basic concept of English grammar. This book talked about the core essentials of English grammar applied in formal English Language. This Grammar book is divided into three (3) essential parts. The first two parts cover Grammar and the final part covers Composition.
html: English is the most common language spoken world-wide and it is one of the easiest languages to learn and speak. This book is all about developing the very basic concept of English grammar. This book talked about the core essentials of English grammar applied in formal English Language. This Grammar book is divided into three (3) essential parts. The first two parts cover Grammar and the final part covers Composition.

plain: The teaching of formal grammar in our school, and the testing of grammar in examination, have both declined in recent years under the influence of our pseudo educationist and innovators of the so-called structural techniques. The old but sturdy edifices seem to be in danger of disappearing, in favour of less structured methods of teaching literacy. The result of this dangerous trend of grammar lessness are now being felt increasingly, because even after ten to twelve years of schooling, the average student is not able to express himself correctly and fluently in the English language. Grammar forms our basis in the learning of any language, and without lying this foundation firmly the so-called ‘structural approach’ is bound to prove futile and even harmful.

Grammar forms our basis in the learning of any language, and without lying this foundation firmly the so-called ‘structural approach’ is bound to prove futile and even harmful.

plain: This book is based on the believe that an understanding of the English language must being with a working knowledge of its components - the parts of speech – and with the way they fit together to form the biggest unit of speech, i.e., the sentence. The knowledge given in this book to the student not as an arid exercise, but as a pleasant and rhythmical drill that will enable the beginner to learn this most important language of the world in an easy and enjoyable manner.

plain: In this book, an attempt has been made to drill the students in the correct use of some typical words, phrases and expressions. Here the stress is not put on the explaining of grammatical names and terms, but on correct usage. It is hoped that this book will help student to achieve the cherished desire of every educated civilian, i.e., the ability to write and speak excellent English.

rows.plain: Subject – Verb Agreement
rows.html: Subject – Verb Agreement

plain: A verb must agree with its Subject in Number and Person. Often, quite wrongly, the Verb is made to agree in number with a noun near it instead of with its proper Subject. This error should be avoided as shown in the following examples:
html: A verb must agree with its Subject in Number and Person. Often, quite wrongly, the Verb is made to agree in number with a noun near it instead of with its proper Subject. This error should be avoided as shown in the following examples:

plain: The quality of mangos was not good.
html: The quality of mangos was not good.

plain: The results of your work are not satisfactory.
html: The results of your work are not satisfactory.

plain: One of the boys was absent yesterday.
html: One of the boys was absent yesterday.

plain: The introduction of new law has improved the situation.
html: The introduction of new law has improved the situation.

plain: His knowledge of Indian language was extraordinary.
html: His knowledge of Indian language was extraordinary.

plain: When the subject (though singular in form) taken together as a whole is plural in meaning, the plural verb should be used; as,
html: When the subject (though singular in form) taken together as a whole is plural in meaning, the plural verb should be used; as,

plain: A large number of students have failed.
html: A large number of students have failed.

plain: The majority of voters are illiterate.
html: The majority of voters are illiterate.

plain: More than twenty boys have complained against him.
html: More than twenty boys have complained against him.

plain: A lot of trees have been cut down.
html: A lot of trees have been cut down.

plain: Two or more singular nouns or pronouns joined by and require a plural verb; as,
html: Two or more singular nouns or pronouns joined by and require a plural verb; as,

plain: He and I were playing.
html: He and I were playing.

plain: Gold and silver are precious metals.
html: Gold and silver are precious metals.

plain: Fire and water do not agree.
html: Fire and water do not agree.

plain: Knowledge and wisdom often have no connection.
html: Knowledge and wisdom often have no connection.

plain: Are you father and mother as home?
html: Are you father and mother as home?

plain: But if the nouns suggest one idea, or refer to the same person or thing, the verb is singular; as,
html: But if the nouns suggest one idea, or refer to the same person or thing, the verb is singular; as,

plain: The horse and carriage is at the door.
html: The horse and carriage is at the door.

plain: Bread and butter is his only food.
html: Bread and butter is his only food.

plain: Honour and glory is your reward.
html: Honour and glory is your reward.

plain: Time and tide waits for no man.
html: Time and tide waits for no man.

plain: The novelist and poet is dead.
html: The novelist and poet is dead.

plain: My friend and guide has come.
html: My friend and guide has come.

plain: The rise and fall of the tide is due to the influence of the moon.
html: The rise and fall of the tide is due to the influence of the moon.

plain: Sari and blouse is the most common dress of Indian women.
html: Sari and blouse is the most common dress of Indian women.

plain: However, when the nouns in a subject are thought of as different units, the plural verb is used; as
html: However, when the nouns in a subject are thought of as different units, the plural verb is used; as

plain: Bread and butter are charged separately.
html: Bread and butter are charged separately.

plain: Sari and blouse are two pieces of a female dress.
html: Sari and blouse are two pieces of a female dress.

plain: His secretary and his typist are on leave today. (Two persons)
html: His secretary and his typist are on leave today. (Two persons)

plain: Two or more singular subjects, connected by ‘or’ or ‘nor’ take a singular verb; as,
html: Two or more singular subjects, connected by ‘or’ or ‘nor’ take a singular verb; as,

plain: No girl or boy was punished.
html: No girl or boy was punished.

plain: Our happiness or our sorrow is the result of our own actions.
html: Our happiness or our sorrow is the result of our own actions.

plain: Either the cat or the dog has spoiled it.
html: Either the cat or the dog has spoiled it.

plain: Neither food nor water was available there.
html: Neither food nor water was available there.

plain: Neither your father nor your mother seems to be happy.
html: Neither your father nor your mother seems to be happy.

plain: But when one of the subjects joined by ‘or’ or ‘nor’ is plural, the verb must be plural, and the plural subject should be placed nearest the verb; as,
html: But when one of the subjects joined by ‘or’ or ‘nor’ is plural, the verb must be plural, and the plural subject should be placed nearest the verb; as,

plain: Neither the chairman nor the directors were present.
html: Neither the chairman nor the directors were present.

plain: This girl or those boys know the secret.
html: This girl or those boys know the secret.

plain: Neither milk nor eggs were available there.
html: Neither milk nor eggs were available there.

plain: When the subject joined by ‘or’ or ‘nor’ are of different persons, the verb agrees with the nearer one; as,
html: When the subject joined by ‘or’ or ‘nor’ are of different persons, the verb agrees with the nearer one; as,

plain: Either he or I am mistaken.
html: Either he or I am mistaken.

plain: Either you or he is mistaken.
html: Either you or he is mistaken.

plain: Neither she nor you are to blame.
html: Neither she nor you are to blame.

plain: Neither my friend nor I am to blame.
html: Neither my friend nor I am to blame.

plain: But it is always better to avoid such constructions, and to write –
html: But it is always better to avoid such constructions, and to write –

plain: He is mistaken or else I am.
html: He is mistaken or else I am.

plain: She is not to blame, nor are you.
html: She is not to blame, nor are you.

plain: The following words must be followed by a singular verb:
html: The following words must be followed by a singular verb:

plain: Two nouns qualified by ‘each’ or ‘every’, even though connected by ‘and’ take a singular verb; as,
html: Two nouns qualified by ‘each’ or ‘every’, even though connected by ‘and’ take a singular verb; as,

html: ‘None’ is constructed in the singular or plural as the sense may require; as,

html: Did you buy any mangoes? There were none in the market.

plain: Have you brought me a letter? There was none for you.

plain: When the singular or plural equally well express the sense, the plural commonly used; as,
html: When the singular or plural equally well express the sense, the plural commonly used; as,

html: The following words always taken a plural verb:

plain: both, few, a few, many, several.

plain: A collective noun takes a singular verb when the collection is thought of as one whole. It takes a plural verb when the individual members of the group are thought of.

plain: A phrase which is the proper name of the book, organisation, country etc. takes a singular verb, even though there may be a plural noun in the phrase; as,
html: A phrase which is the proper name of the book, organisation, country etc. takes a singular verb, even though there may be a plural noun in the phrase; as,

plain: Phrases which express an amount, a quantity, a period of time or a unit of distance take singular verb, even though the phrases may have plural noun in them; as
html: Phrases which express an amount, a quantity, a period of time or a unit of distance take singular verb, even though the phrases may have plural noun in them; as

plain: Fifty thousand rupees is a big sum.

plain: Three parts of the work has been finished.
html: Three parts of the work has been finished.

plain: One hundred paise is equal to one rupee.
html: One hundred paise is equal to one rupee.

plain: However, when the amount, quantity or period is considered as a number of separate units, the subject takes a plural verb; as
html: However, when the amount, quantity or period is considered as a number of separate units, the subject takes a plural verb; as

plain: Fifty silver rupees were found in the old man’s box.
html: Fifty silver rupees were found in the old man’s box.

plain: The last two years have been a hard time for me.
html: The last two years have been a hard time for me.

plain: Five kilometres of this road need repairs.

plain: Two hundred acres have been wasted by floods.

plain: Some nouns which are plural in form, but singular in meaning, takes a singular verb; as,
html: Some nouns which are plural in form, but singular in meaning, takes a singular verb; as,

plain: Politics was a business of his life.

plain: The wages of sin is death.

plain: ‘Pains’ and ‘means’ can take singular or plural verb, but the construction must be consistent; as,
html: ‘Pains’ and ‘means’ can take singular or plural verb, but the construction must be consistent; as,

html: Great pains has/have been taken.

plain: The means employed by you is/are unfair.
html: The means employed by you is/are unfair.

plain: All possible means have been employed.
html: All possible means have been employed.

plain: The verb in a sentence agrees with the number of the subject and not with the number of the noun that is added to it by using such words as with, as well as, in addition to, together with, accompanied by, etc.
html: The verb in a sentence agrees with the number of the subject and not with the number of the noun that is added to it by using such words as with, as well as, in addition to, together with, accompanied by, etc.

html: The ship, with its crew, was lost.

plain: The Principal, together with his staff, was present.
html: The Principal, together with his staff, was present.

plain: The house, in addition to its contents, was sold
html: The house, in addition to its contents, was sold

rows.html: 02

rows.plain: The Correct use of Nouns
rows.html: The Correct use of Nouns

plain: Some points have already been discussed in the first chapter about the correct use if nouns in the Subjective (or Nominative) case. Here we shall discuss their use in Possessive case.
html: Some points have already been discussed in the first chapter about the correct use if nouns in the Subjective (or Nominative) case. Here we shall discuss their use in Possessive case.

plain: 20.	The Possessive case of a noun is used to show ownership or possession. It answers the question, ‘whose?’
html: 20. The Possessive case of a noun is used to show ownership or possession. It answers the question, ‘whose?’

plain: Whose Umbrella?	 –	Rama’s
html: Whose Umbrella? – Rama’s

plain: But the Possessive case does not always denote possession. It is used to denote authorship, origin, kind, etc.; as,
html: But the Possessive case does not always denote possession. It is used to denote authorship, origin, kind, etc.; as,

plain: Shakespeare’s plays		= the plays written by Shakespeare
html: Shakespeare’s plays		= the plays written by Shakespeare

plain: A mother’s love		= the love felt by a mother.
html: A mother’s love		= the love felt by a mother.

plain: Mr. Paul’s speech		= the speech delivered by Mr. Paul.
html: Mr. Paul’s speech		= the speech delivered by Mr. Paul.

plain: The court’s order		= the order passed by the court.
html: The court’s order		= the order passed by the court.

plain: Alexander’s victories		= the victories scored by Alexander.
html: Alexander’s victories		= the victories scored by Alexander.

plain: Rama’s temple		= the temple dedicated to Rama.
html: Rama’s temple		= the temple dedicated to Rama.

plain: Birla’s temple			= the temple built by Birla.
html: Birla’s temple			= the temple built by Birla.

plain: Formation of the Possessive Case
html: Formation of the Possessive Case

plain: When the noun is singular, the possessive case is formed by adding’s (which is called apostrophe s) the noun; as,
html: When the noun is singular, the possessive case is formed by adding’s (which is called apostrophe s) the noun; as,

plain: The boy’s book; the king’s crown.
html: The boy’s book; the king’s crown.

plain: When the noun is plural, and ends in s, the possessive is formed by adding only an apostrophe; as,
html: When the noun is plural, and ends in s, the possessive is formed by adding only an apostrophe; as,

plain: Boys’ school; girls’ college; horses’ tail
html: Boys’ school; girls’ college; horses’ tail

plain: When the noun is plural, and does not end in s, the possessive is formed by adding’s; as,
html: When the noun is plural, and does not end in s, the possessive is formed by adding’s; as,

plain: Men’s club; children’s park.
html: Men’s club; children’s park.

plain: When a noun or title consists of several words, the possessive sign is put to the last word only; as,
html: When a noun or title consists of several words, the possessive sign is put to the last word only; as,

plain: The Nawab of Rampur’s library.
html: The Nawab of Rampur’s library.

plain: I saw it at Johnson and Nicholson’s
html: I saw it at Johnson and Nicholson’s

plain: When two nouns are in apposition, the possessive sign is put to the later only; as,
html: When two nouns are in apposition, the possessive sign is put to the later only; as,

plain: This is Tagore, the poet’s house.
html: This is Tagore, the poet’s house.

plain: We went to Lilaram, the jeweller’s shop.
html: We went to Lilaram, the jeweller’s shop.

plain: Also, when two nouns are closely connected, the possessive is put to the latter; as,
html: Also, when two nouns are closely connected, the possessive is put to the latter; as,

plain: Huntley and Palmer’s biscuits.
html: Huntley and Palmer’s biscuits.

plain: William and Mary’s reign.
html: William and Mary’s reign.

plain: Two or more connected nouns implying separate possession must take the possessive sign separately; as,
html: Two or more connected nouns implying separate possession must take the possessive sign separately; as,

plain: Dalip’s and Amitabh’s acting.
html: Dalip’s and Amitabh’s acting.

plain: Goldsmith’s and Cowper’s poems.
html: Goldsmith’s and Cowper’s poems.

plain: Use of the Possessive Case
html: Use of the Possessive Case

plain: The possessive case is used with
html: The possessive case is used with

plain: Nouns denoting living things; as,
html: Nouns denoting living things; as,

plain: Manab’s book; a man’s face; a cat’s tail; a horse’s head; a bird’s feathers; a dog’s paws.
html: Manab’s book; a man’s face; a cat’s tail; a horse’s head; a bird’s feathers; a dog’s paws.

plain: Nouns denoting personified things; as,
html: Nouns denoting personified things; as,

plain: India’s heroes; Nature’s law; duty’s call; death’s door.
html: India’s heroes; Nature’s law; duty’s call; death’s door.

plain: Nouns denoting certain dignified objects; as,
html: Nouns denoting certain dignified objects; as,

plain: The court’s order; the sun’s ray; the world’s population; the Earth’s atmosphere; the soul’s call; the country’s good; the mind’s eye.
html: The court’s order; the sun’s ray; the world’s population; the Earth’s atmosphere; the soul’s call; the country’s good; the mind’s eye.

plain: Nouns denoting time, space, weight or value; as,
html: Nouns denoting time, space, weight or value; as,

plain: TIME	– A day’s journey; a month’s holiday; three weeks’ leave; a year’s     absence; in two hours’ time.
html: TIME – A day’s journey; a month’s holiday; three weeks’ leave; a year’s absence; in two hours’ time.

plain: SPACE	– A boat’s length; a hair’s breadth; a stone’s throw; a needle’s point.
html: SPACE – A boat’s length; a hair’s breadth; a stone’s throw; a needle’s point.

plain: WETGHT	– A pound’s weight; a ton’s weight.
html: WETGHT – A pound’s weight; a ton’s weight.

plain: VALUE	– ten rupees’ worth; a shilling’s worth.
html: VALUE – ten rupees’ worth; a shilling’s worth.

plain: In certain phrases, for the sake of shortness; as,
html: In certain phrases, for the sake of shortness; as,

plain: At his fingers’ end; for mercy’s sake; to his heart’s content; at his wit’s end; a boat’s crew; the ship’s passengers; to his journey’s end.
html: At his fingers’ end; for mercy’s sake; to his heart’s content; at his wit’s end; a boat’s crew; the ship’s passengers; to his journey’s end.

plain: A noun denoting some kind of place or building is sometimes omitted after a noun in the possessive case; as,
html: A noun denoting some kind of place or building is sometimes omitted after a noun in the possessive case; as,

plain: Tonight; I dine at my uncle’s. (house)
html: Tonight; I dine at my uncle’s. (house)

plain: I’ll see you at the barber’s. (shop)
html: I’ll see you at the barber’s. (shop)

plain: He was educated at Queen’s. (college)
html: He was educated at Queen’s. (college)

plain: Look at the following phrases:
html: Look at the following phrases:

plain: a friend of Mohan’s	= one of Mohan’s friends
html: a friend of Mohan’s = one of Mohan’s friends

plain: a play of Shakespeare’s    = one of the plays of Shakespeare.
html: a play of Shakespeare’s = one of the plays of Shakespeare.

plain: Note that in such phrases, the second noun denotes a person, not a thing. But we cannot say, for example,
html: Note that in such phrases, the second noun denotes a person, not a thing. But we cannot say, for example,

plain: a leg of the chair’s
html: a leg of the chair’s

plain: Also, note that the first noun in such phrases has a(n) before it, not ‘the’. We cannot say, for example,
html: Also, note that the first noun in such phrases has a(n) before it, not ‘the’. We cannot say, for example,

plain: the friend of Mohan’s.
html: the friend of Mohan’s.

plain: Nouns denoting lifeless objects are seldom put in the possessive case. So, we must say–
html: Nouns denoting lifeless objects are seldom put in the possessive case. So, we must say–

plain: The legs of the table [not, the table’s leg]
html: The legs of the table [not, the table’s leg]

plain: The cover of the book [not, the book’s cover]
html: The cover of the book [not, the book’s cover]

plain: The roof of the house [not, the house’s roof].
html: The roof of the house [not, the house’s roof].

plain: Relation in such cases is indicated by the preposition ‘of’; or the Noun can sometimes be used as if it were as Adjective.
html: Relation in such cases is indicated by the preposition ‘of’; or the Noun can sometimes be used as if it were as Adjective.

plain: The door of the cottage	= the cottage door.
html: The door of the cottage	= the cottage door.

plain: The roads of the city		= the city roads
html: The roads of the city		= the city roads

plain: The flowers of summer	= the summer flowers
html: The flowers of summer	= the summer flowers

plain: The Singular & Plural use of Nouns
html: The Singular & Plural use of Nouns

plain: The following nouns are used only in the singular and take a singular verb:
html: The following nouns are used only in the singular and take a singular verb:

plain: Furniture, scenery, luggage, machinery, information, advice, bread, poetry, hair, business, mischief, issue, offspring (children).
html: Furniture, scenery, luggage, machinery, information, advice, bread, poetry, hair, business, mischief, issue, offspring (children).

plain: But the word ‘hair’ is used in Plural when a definite number of hairs are to be mentioned.
html: But the word ‘hair’ is used in Plural when a definite number of hairs are to be mentioned.

plain: My hair has turned grey.
html: My hair has turned grey.

plain: Two of my hairs have turned grey.
html: Two of my hairs have turned grey.

plain: The following nouns have plural form but they are used in the singular sense and take a singular verb:
html: The following nouns have plural form but they are used in the singular sense and take a singular verb:

plain: Innings, wages, politics, gallows, means, summons, news, mathematics, physics, economics.
html: Innings, wages, politics, gallows, means, summons, news, mathematics, physics, economics.

plain: We won by an innings and two runs.
html: We won by an innings and two runs.

plain: Mathematics is my favourite subject.
html: Mathematics is my favourite subject.

plain: The following nouns are never used in the singular form; they always take a plural verb:
html: The following nouns are never used in the singular form; they always take a plural verb:

plain: Alms, riches, contents, circumstances, thanks, means.

plain: Alms were given to the poor.

plain: Riches have turned his head.

plain: Some articles have two almost similar parts. The plural form of the noun is used for such articles, and they always take a plural verb.

plain: Scissors, tongs, spectacles, trousers, breeches.

plain: Some collective nouns, though singular in form, are always used as plurals; as,

rows.plain: The Correct use of Pronouns
rows.html: The Correct use of Pronouns

plain: My, our, your, his, her, there are possessive Adjectives; they are used with nouns. Mine, ours, yours, hers, theirs are possessive Pronouns; they are used in place of nouns (not with nouns).
html: My, our, your, his, her, there are possessive Adjectives; they are used with nouns. Mine, ours, yours, hers, theirs are possessive Pronouns; they are used in place of nouns (not with nouns).

plain: This is my book.
html: This is my book.

plain: This book is mine. (We can’t say: This is mine book.)
html: This book is mine. (We can’t say: This is mine book.)

plain: My horse and yours (your horse) are both tired.
html: My horse and yours (your horse) are both tired.

plain: I will get my share and she will get hers (her share).
html: I will get my share and she will get hers (her share).

plain: The apostrophe ( ’ ) is not used with the following Pronouns:
html: The apostrophe ( ’ ) is not used with the following Pronouns:

plain: Ours, yours, hers, its, theirs.					(it’s = it is)
html: Ours, yours, hers, its, theirs. (it’s = it is)

plain: Incorrect Use			Correct Use
html: Incorrect Use Correct Use

plain: Your’s sincerely			Yours sincerely
html: Your’s sincerely Yours sincerely

plain: These ideas of our’s			These ideas of ours.
html: These ideas of our’s These ideas of ours.

plain: This action of her’s			This action of hers.
html: This action of her’s This action of hers.

plain: It’s shape and size			Its shape and size
html: It’s shape and size Its shape and size

plain: These houses of their’s		These houses of theirs
html: These houses of their’s These houses of theirs

plain: The complement of the verb ‘to be’ (is, am, are, was, were, etc.) is always in the Subjective form (i.e., I, we, he, she, they).
html: The complement of the verb ‘to be’ (is, am, are, was, were, etc.) is always in the Subjective form (i.e., I, we, he, she, they).

plain: It was he (not him).
html: It was he (not him).

plain: If I were he I wouldn’t do it.
html: If I were he I wouldn’t do it.

plain: I am she (not her) who taught him a lesson.
html: I am she (not her) who taught him a lesson.

plain: It is I (not me) that told the truth.
html: It is I (not me) that told the truth.

plain: An Adjective is a word that modifies a noun or pronoun. Adjectives describe about the noun or pronoun. ‘This’ and ‘that’ are followed by singular nouns and ‘these’ and ‘those’ are followed by plural nouns.
html: An Adjective is a word that modifies a noun or pronoun. Adjectives describe about the noun or pronoun. ‘This’ and ‘that’ are followed by singular nouns and ‘these’ and ‘those’ are followed by plural nouns.

plain: Mistakes are often made by using 'these' and ‘those’ with singular nouns. The following sentences are incorrect:

plain: The words 'superior', 'inferior', 'senior', 'junior' are always used as a comparative degree but they are followed by 'to' instead of 'than':

plain: This cloth is superior/inferior to that.

plain: He is junior/senior to me in age.
html: He is junior/senior to me in age.

plain: In comparing two things, the Comparative (not the Superlative) should be used; as,
html: In comparing two things, the Comparative (not the Superlative) should be used; as,

plain: He is the taller of the two.
html: He is the taller of the two.

plain: Which is the better (not best) of the two?
html: Which is the better (not best) of the two?

plain: Of the two evils, choose the less (not least).
html: Of the two evils, choose the less (not least).

plain: When one thing is compared with every other of the same kind, take care to use 'any other' after an adjective in the Positive or Comparative degree; as,
html: When one thing is compared with every other of the same kind, take care to use 'any other' after an adjective in the Positive or Comparative degree; as,

html: But ‘other’ must not be used after a Superlative degree:

plain: He was the best of all leaders. (correct)

plain: When a thing or person is to be compared with another thing or person of the same kind, we can use the Positive degree or the Comparative degree.

plain: She is not so wise as her sister. 	(Positive)

plain: Her sister is wiser than she (is)	(Comparative)
html: Her sister is wiser than she (is) (Comparative)

plain: It is clear from the above example that an Affirmative sentence in the Positive degree becomes Negative in the Comparative degree, and a Negative sentence in the Positive degree becomes Affirmative in the Comparative degree.

rows.plain: The Correct use of Articles

plain: As a general rule, as article (a/an/the) is used before a singular countable noun.
html: As a general rule, as article (a/an/the) is used before a singular countable noun.

plain: I saw an elephant in the street.
html: I saw an elephant in the street.

plain: I saw the dog in the street.
html: I saw the dog in the street.

plain: The article ‘an’ is used before a vowel, a consonant sounded like a vowel, or a silent h; as,
html: The article ‘an’ is used before a vowel, a consonant sounded like a vowel, or a silent h; as,

plain: an ass, an enemy, an inkpot, an orange, an umbrella, an M.A., an hour, an honest man, an historical building etc.
html: an ass, an enemy, an inkpot, an orange, an umbrella, an M.A., an hour, an honest man, an historical building etc.

plain: The article ‘a’ is used before a consonant, before u sounded as yu, and before o sounded as wu; as,
html: The article ‘a’ is used before a consonant, before u sounded as yu, and before o sounded as wu; as,

plain: a boy, a woman, a yard, a house, a hole;

plain: a university, a unicorn, a useful thing, a European;

plain: The article ‘the’ is used –
html: The article ‘the’ is used –

plain: When we speak of a particular person or thing, or one already referred to, or known to the speaker; as

rows.plain: The Correct use of Adverbs
rows.html: The Correct use of Adverbs

plain: An Adverb should be placed as near as possible to the word or words it modifies; as,
html: An Adverb should be placed as near as possible to the word or words it modifies; as,

plain: He had got almost to the top when the rope broke.
html: He had got almost to the top when the rope broke.

plain: Adverbs of Time (always, never, often, sometimes, generally, rarely, seldom, just, etc.) are placed immediately in front of the main verb of the sentence; as,
html: Adverbs of Time (always, never, often, sometimes, generally, rarely, seldom, just, etc.) are placed immediately in front of the main verb of the sentence; as,

plain: I often see him.
html: I often see him.

plain: I have often seen him.
html: I have often seen him.

plain: They are placed after the verb ‘be’; as,
html: They are placed after the verb ‘be’; as,

plain: She is always late.
html: She is always late.

plain: You are never to come late again.
html: You are never to come late again.

plain: Other common adverbs coming in this position are:
html: Other common adverbs coming in this position are:

plain: almost, nearly, quite, hardly, scarcely
html: almost, nearly, quite, hardly, scarcely

plain: The adverb ‘only’ should be placed carefully in a sentence. As a general rule, ‘only’ should be placed immediately before the word it is meant to modify; as,
html: The adverb ‘only’ should be placed carefully in a sentence. As a general rule, ‘only’ should be placed immediately before the word it is meant to modify; as,

plain: I worked only two sums.
html: I worked only two sums.

plain: Only Jolim could tell the truth.
html: Only Jolim could tell the truth.

plain: I praise him only when he works hard.
html: I praise him only when he works hard.

plain: Two negatives destroy each other. Hence two negatives should not be used in the same sentence unless we wish to make an affirmative statement. We should say:
html: Two negatives destroy each other. Hence two negatives should not be used in the same sentence unless we wish to make an affirmative statement. We should say:

plain: I haven't got any (not none).
html: I haven't got any (not none).

plain: I could not find it anywhere (not nowhere).
html: I could not find it anywhere (not nowhere).

plain: I haven't any (not no) homework to do.
html: I haven't any (not no) homework to do.

plain: I can't see any (not no) grace in her.
html: I can't see any (not no) grace in her.

plain: Adjectives should not be used for Adverbs. We should say, for instance:
html: Adjectives should not be used for Adverbs. We should say, for instance:

plain: He returned more quickly (not quicker) than I expected.
html: He returned more quickly (not quicker) than I expected.

plain: He will pay heavily (not heavy) for his mistake.
html: He will pay heavily (not heavy) for his mistake.

plain: 'Ever' is sometimes misused for 'never'.
html: 'Ever' is sometimes misused for 'never'.

plain: We seldom or never (not ever) visit our relatives.
html: We seldom or never (not ever) visit our relatives.

plain: It seldom or never (not ever) rains in this month.
html: It seldom or never (not ever) rains in this month.

plain: Note: ‘Seldom or never’ and ‘seldom if ever’ are both correct but ‘seldom or ever’ is incorrect.
html: Note: ‘Seldom or never’ and ‘seldom if ever’ are both correct but ‘seldom or ever’ is incorrect.

plain: ‘Else’ should be followed by ‘but’.
html: ‘Else’ should be followed by ‘but’.

plain: ‘Other’ should be followed by ‘than’.
html: ‘Other’ should be followed by ‘than’.

plain: ‘Other than’ = except.
html: ‘Other than’ = except.

plain: ‘Other than’ is used after a negative.
html: ‘Other than’ is used after a negative.

plain: It is nothing else but (not else than) pride.
html: It is nothing else but (not else than) pride.

plain: She had no close friends other than him.
html: She had no close friends other than him.

plain: ‘Very’ is used with a Positive degree of Adjective.
html: ‘Very’ is used with a Positive degree of Adjective.

plain: ‘Much’ is used with a Comparative degree of Adjective.
html: ‘Much’ is used with a Comparative degree of Adjective.

plain: She is very happy now.
html: She is very happy now.

plain: She is much happier now.
html: She is much happier now.

plain: The Present Participle (V₁-ing) as an Adjective takes 'very' with it.
html: The Present Participle (V₁-ing) as an Adjective takes 'very' with it.

plain: The Past Participle (V3) as an Adjective takes ‘much’ with it.
html: The Past Participle (V3) as an Adjective takes ‘much’ with it.

plain: It is very surprising.
html: It is very surprising.

plain: I am much worried about her.
html: I am much worried about her.

plain: She has a very charming face.
html: She has a very charming face.

plain: It is a much discussed topic.
html: It is a much discussed topic.

plain: Note: A few Past Participles take ‘very’ before them; as,
html: Note: A few Past Participles take ‘very’ before them; as,

plain: I am very pleased to hear this.
html: I am very pleased to hear this.

plain: I am very tired now.
html: I am very tired now.

plain: The adverb ‘too’ means ‘more than enough’. It should not be used in the sense of ‘very’ or ‘much’.
html: The adverb ‘too’ means ‘more than enough’. It should not be used in the sense of ‘very’ or ‘much’.

plain: I am too pleased with you. 			(incorrect)
html: I am too pleased with you. (incorrect)

plain: I am very pleased with you. 			(correct)
html: I am very pleased with you. (correct)

plain: She is too interested in music. 		(incorrect)
html: She is too interested in music. (incorrect)

plain: She is much interested in music.		(correct)
html: She is much interested in music. (correct)

plain: ‘Too’ and ‘enough’ are opposite in meaning.
html: ‘Too’ and ‘enough’ are opposite in meaning.

plain: ‘Too’ is used before the word it qualifies.
html: ‘Too’ is used before the word it qualifies.

plain: ‘Enough’ (as an adverb) is used after the word it qualifies.
html: ‘Enough’ (as an adverb) is used after the word it qualifies.

plain: He is too weak to walk.
html: He is too weak to walk.

plain: I am too late for the train.
html: I am too late for the train.

plain: Mangal is rich enough to buy a car.
html: Mangal is rich enough to buy a car.

plain: She is intelligent enough to understand it.
html: She is intelligent enough to understand it.

plain: The adverb ‘never’ can't be used with verbs indicating a special point of time.
html: The adverb ‘never’ can't be used with verbs indicating a special point of time.

plain: ‘Never’ can't take the place of ‘not’.
html: ‘Never’ can't take the place of ‘not’.

plain: I never remember having met her. 			(incorrect)
html: I never remember having met her. (incorrect)

plain: I don't remember ever having met her. 		(correct)
html: I don't remember ever having met her. (correct)

plain: Kipling was never born in London. 			(incorrect)
html: Kipling was never born in London. (incorrect)

plain: Kipling was not born in London. 			(correct)
html: Kipling was not born in London. (correct)

plain: We met the other day, but he never talked of it. 	(incorrect)
html: We met the other day, but he never talked of it. (incorrect)

plain: We met the other day, but he didn't talk of it.	(correct)
html: We met the other day, but he didn't talk of it. (correct)

plain: Do not use ‘quite’ in the sense of ‘very’ or ‘to a considerable degree’.
html: Do not use ‘quite’ in the sense of ‘very’ or ‘to a considerable degree’.

plain: I am very (not quite) sorry to hear the sad news.
html: I am very (not quite) sorry to hear the sad news.

plain: Little = not much
html: Little = not much

plain: It is use in the negative sense.
html: It is use in the negative sense.

plain: A little = some at least.
html: A little = some at least.

plain: It is used in the affirmative sense.
html: It is used in the affirmative sense.

plain: I little expected that he would sense.
html: I little expected that he would sense.

plain: I am feeling a little tired.
html: I am feeling a little tired.

rows.plain: The Correct use of Prepositions
rows.html: The Correct use of Prepositions

plain: ‘In’ is used with names of countries and large towns; ‘at’ is more often used when speaking of small towns and villages; as,
html: ‘In’ is used with names of countries and large towns; ‘at’ is more often used when speaking of small towns and villages; as,

plain: He is in America. They live in Delhi.
html: He is in America. They live in Delhi.

plain: He lives at Saket in Delhi.
html: He lives at Saket in Delhi.

plain: ‘In’ and ‘at’ are used in speaking of things at rest; ‘to’ and ‘into’ are used in speaking of things in motion; as,
html: ‘In’ and ‘at’ are used in speaking of things at rest; ‘to’ and ‘into’ are used in speaking of things in motion; as,

plain: He is in bed.
html: He is in bed.

plain: He is at the top of the class.
html: He is at the top of the class.

plain: He ran to school.
html: He ran to school.

plain: He jumped into the river.
html: He jumped into the river.

plain: The snake crawled into its hole.
html: The snake crawled into its hole.

plain: ‘On’ is often used in speaking of things at rest; ‘upon’ is used of things in motion; as,
html: ‘On’ is often used in speaking of things at rest; ‘upon’ is used of things in motion; as,

plain: He sat on a chair.
html: He sat on a chair.

plain: The cat sprang upon the table.
html: The cat sprang upon the table.

plain: ‘Till’ is used of time; ‘to’ is used of place; as,
html: ‘Till’ is used of time; ‘to’ is used of place; as,

plain: I slept till eight O’ clock.
html: I slept till eight O’ clock.

plain: He walked to the end of the street.
html: He walked to the end of the street.

plain: ‘with’ denotes the instruments; ‘by’ denotes the agent; as
html: ‘with’ denotes the instruments; ‘by’ denotes the agent; as

plain: He killed two birds with one shot.
html: He killed two birds with one shot.

plain: He was stabbed by his enemy with a dragger.
html: He was stabbed by his enemy with a dragger.

plain: ‘Since’ is used with a perfect tense; ‘from’ is used with all tenses; as,
html: ‘Since’ is used with a perfect tense; ‘from’ is used with all tenses; as,

plain: I have eaten nothing since yesterday.
html: I have eaten nothing since yesterday.

plain: He has been ill since Monday last.
html: He has been ill since Monday last.

plain: I have not seen him since last week.
html: I have not seen him since last week.

plain: I started work from fifteenth January.
html: I started work from fifteenth January.

plain: I shall start working from tomorrow.
html: I shall start working from tomorrow.

plain: Kindly grant me leave from today.
html: Kindly grant me leave from today.

plain: ‘Between’ is used with two persons or things, ‘among’ is used with more than two persons or things.
html: ‘Between’ is used with two persons or things, ‘among’ is used with more than two persons or things.

html: Between nose and eyes, a strange contest arose.

html: The two brothers shared the property between themselves.

html: The villagers often quarrelled among themselves.

html: Cow-worship is popular among the Hindus.

plain: ‘Beside’ means by the side of,
html: ‘Beside’ means by the side of,

plain: ‘Besides’ means in addition to.
html: ‘Besides’ means in addition to.

html: She came and sat beside me.

html: There will be five of us for dinner, besides John.

html: The play was badly acted, besides being too long.

rows.plain: The Correct use of Conjunctions
rows.html: The Correct use of Conjunctions

plain: ‘No sooner’ is always followed by ‘than’, not by ‘but’.
html: ‘No sooner’ is always followed by ‘than’, not by ‘but’.

plain: Scarcely (or Hardly) is always followed by ‘when’, not by ‘than’.
html: Scarcely (or Hardly) is always followed by ‘when’, not by ‘than’.

plain: No sooner did he return than (not but) he was off again.
html: No sooner did he return than (not but) he was off again.

plain: Scarcely had he gone when (not than) a policeman knocked at the door.
html: Scarcely had he gone when (not than) a policeman knocked at the door.

plain: She had hardly heard the news when she began to weep.
html: She had hardly heard the news when she began to weep.

plain: ‘Unless’ means ‘if not’. It means never be confused with ‘if’.
html: ‘Unless’ means ‘if not’. It means never be confused with ‘if’.

plain: Never use ‘not’ with ‘unless’
html: Never use ‘not’ with ‘unless’

plain: ‘If’ can be followed by ‘not’ if the sense demands it.
html: ‘If’ can be followed by ‘not’ if the sense demands it.

plain: Unless you don not work hard, you cannot pass.		(incorrect)
html: Unless you don not work hard, you cannot pass.		(incorrect)

plain: Unless you work hard, you cannot pass.			(correct)
html: Unless you work hard, you cannot pass.			(correct)

plain: If you don’t work hard, you cannot pass.			(correct)
html: If you don’t work hard, you cannot pass.			(correct)

plain: Until (till) = up to the time when.
html: Until (till) = up to the time when.

plain: Both of these words denote some point of time.
html: Both of these words denote some point of time.

plain: ‘Until’ is preferred in the initial position.
html: ‘Until’ is preferred in the initial position.

plain: Go straight on till/until you come to the Post Office and then turn left.
html: Go straight on till/until you come to the Post Office and then turn left.

plain: She won’t go away till/until you give her something.
html: She won’t go away till/until you give her something.

plain: Until you told me, I had heard nothing of it.
html: Until you told me, I had heard nothing of it.

plain: So long as, as long as, while = during the time when.
html: So long as, as long as, while = during the time when.

plain: These words denote period of time.
html: These words denote period of time.

plain: While there is life, there is hope.
html: While there is life, there is hope.

plain: As long as she lived here, she was a problem to me.
html: As long as she lived here, she was a problem to me.

plain: You need not fear anymore so long as you work honestly.
html: You need not fear anymore so long as you work honestly.

plain: The following examples will clear all doubts, read them carefully:
html: The following examples will clear all doubts, read them carefully:

plain: Incorrect					Correct
html: Incorrect Correct

rows.plain: Until you work hard, you will improve.
rows.html: Until you work hard, you will improve.

rows.plain: As long as you work hard, you will improve.
rows.html: As long as you work hard, you will improve.

rows.plain: He continued studying as long as he was forty years old.
rows.html: He continued studying as long as he was forty years old.

rows.plain: He continued studying till he was forty.
rows.html: He continued studying till he was forty.

rows.plain: Until it rains, we must stay inside the house.
rows.html: Until it rains, we must stay inside the house.

rows.plain: As long as it rains, we must stay inside the house.
rows.html: As long as it rains, we must stay inside the house.

plain: ‘Because’ is used to express a cause or reason.
html: ‘Because’ is used to express a cause or reason.

plain: ‘So that’ is used to express a purpose.
html: ‘So that’ is used to express a purpose.

plain: He failed because he did not work hard.			(incorrect)
html: He failed because he did not work hard. (incorrect)

plain: He worked hard so that he might win a scholarship.	(correct)
html: He worked hard so that he might win a scholarship. (correct)

plain: Men work because they may earn a living.			(incorrect)
html: Men work because they may earn a living. (incorrect)

plain: Men work so that they may earn a living.			(correct)
html: Men work so that they may earn a living. (correct)

plain: He took medicine because he might get well.		(incorrect)
html: He took medicine because he might get well. (incorrect)

plain: He took medicine so that he might get well.		(correct)
html: He took medicine so that he might get well. (correct)

plain: ‘Since’ never takes the Past Simple (V2) before it.
html: ‘Since’ never takes the Past Simple (V2) before it.

plain: ‘Since’ always takes the Past Simple (V2) after it.
html: ‘Since’ always takes the Past Simple (V2) after it.

plain: Two months passed since he has come here.
html: Two months passed since he has come here.

plain: Two months have passed since he come here.
html: Two months have passed since he come here.

plain: It was a week since my holidays had begun.
html: It was a week since my holidays had begun.

plain: It is a week since my holiday begun.
html: It is a week since my holiday begun.

plain: Two hours passed since he had fallen asleep.
html: Two hours passed since he had fallen asleep.

plain: Two hours have passed since he fell asleep.
html: Two hours have passed since he fell asleep.

plain: ‘Before’, ‘if’, ‘until’, ‘unless’, ‘while’, when’, etc. are Conjunctions of time.
html: ‘Before’, ‘if’, ‘until’, ‘unless’, ‘while’, when’, etc. are Conjunctions of time.

plain: They are never followed by a verb in the Future tense.
html: They are never followed by a verb in the Future tense.

plain: I will help him if he comes (not will come) here.
html: I will help him if he comes (not will come) here.

plain: The crops will fail before the rain fall (not will fall).
html: The crops will fail before the rain fall (not will fall).

plain: I shall not let you go unless you (not will pay) back my money.
html: I shall not let you go unless you (not will pay) back my money.

plain: I shall give him your message when he reaches (not will reach) home.
html: I shall give him your message when he reaches (not will reach) home.

plain: ‘That’ is never used before a sentence in Direct Narration. It is also used before Interrogative links in Indirect Narration.
html: ‘That’ is never used before a sentence in Direct Narration. It is also used before Interrogative links in Indirect Narration.

plain: He said that ‘I shall be soon here’.				(incorrect)
html: He said that ‘I shall be soon here’. (incorrect)

plain: He said, I shall be soon here’.				(correct)
html: He said, I shall be soon here’.				(correct)

plain: He asked that if Manu had passed.				(incorrect)
html: He asked that if Manu had passed. (incorrect)

plain: He asked if Manu had passed.				(correct)
html: He asked if Manu had passed. (correct)

plain: I wish to know that when he will come.			(incorrect)
html: I wish to know that when he will come. (incorrect)

plain: I wish to know when he will come.				(correct)
html: I wish to know when he will come. (correct)

plain: ‘Lest’ is always followed by the auxiliary ‘should’.
html: ‘Lest’ is always followed by the auxiliary ‘should’.

plain: He worked hard lest he should fail.
html: He worked hard lest he should fail.

plain: Walk fast lest you miss the train.				(incorrect)
html: Walk fast lest you miss the train. (incorrect)

plain: Walk fast lest you should miss the train.			(correct)
html: Walk fast lest you should miss the train. (correct)

plain: The word ‘like’ is not a Conjunction. It is a Preposition and should not be confused with ‘as’ which is a Conjunction.
html: The word ‘like’ is not a Conjunction. It is a Preposition and should not be confused with ‘as’ which is a Conjunction.

plain: Do it like I do.							(incorrect)
html: Do it like I do. (incorrect)

plain: Do it as I do.							(correct)
html: Do it as I do. (correct)

plain: She sings like her mother does.				(incorrect)
html: She sings like her mother does. (incorrect)

plain: She sings as her mother does.				(correct)
html: She sings as her mother does. (correct)

plain: ‘No other’ and ‘rather’ are followed by ‘than’
html: ‘No other’ and ‘rather’ are followed by ‘than’

plain: No other than Mohan did this Mischief.
html: No other than Mohan did this Mischief.

plain: I would rather starve than beg.
html: I would rather starve than beg.

plain: As to = about concerning
html: As to = about concerning

plain: Supposing = if
html: Supposing = if

plain: I don’t know as to why she came here.			(incorrect)
html: I don’t know as to why she came here. (incorrect)

plain: I don’t know why she came here.				(correct)
html: I don’t know why she came here. (correct)

plain: Supposing if he fails, what will he do?			(incorrect)
html: Supposing if he fails, what will he do? (incorrect)

plain: Supposing he fails, what will he do?				(correct)
html: Supposing he fails, what will he do? (correct)

plain: When a negative is used at the beginning of a sentence, its negative influence spreads over the whole sentence. Therefore, the negative ‘not’ and ‘never’ must be followed by ‘or’, not ‘nor’.
html: When a negative is used at the beginning of a sentence, its negative influence spreads over the whole sentence. Therefore, the negative ‘not’ and ‘never’ must be followed by ‘or’, not ‘nor’.

plain: I have never spoken to her nor written to her.		(incorrect)
html: I have never spoken to her nor written to her. (incorrect)

plain: I have never spoken to her or written to her.		(correct)
html: I have never spoken to her or written to her. (correct)

plain: She did not break it nor lose it.				(incorrect)
html: She did not break it nor lose it. (incorrect)

plain: He did not break it or lose it.				(correct)
html: He did not break it or lose it. (correct)

plain: The Conjunction ‘neither’ must always be followed by ‘nor’
html: The Conjunction ‘neither’ must always be followed by ‘nor’

plain: I have neither written to her or speak to her.		(incorrect)
html: I have neither written to her or speak to her. (incorrect)

plain: I have neither written to her nor speak to her.		(correct)
html: I have neither written to her nor speak to her. (correct)

plain: He watched neither his hands or his feet.			(incorrect)
html: He watched neither his hands or his feet. (incorrect)

plain: He watched neither his hand nor his feet.			(correct)
html: He watched neither his hand nor his feet. (correct)

plain: Some Conjunction go in pairs, e.g.

html: either ………. or

html: neither ………. Nor

html: both ………….. and

html: not only ………… but also.
